text:
How did we go from a glut but not a glut so quickly? It's all about the trading cycle of crude and I call it The Asian Crude Buying Conundrum. 

Read the slide for more details because a picture speaks a thousand words.

#oott

BREAKING: Houthis of Yemen claim they attacked two tankers carrying Saudi oil in the Red Sea. Separately, @UK_MTO just reported a tanker (potentially one of the two mentioned above) was hit by projectiles and is fighting a fire near the mouth of the Bab el Mandeb strait.

Iranian top negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf:

“…The equation of this war is clear: either all or none! In a region where we do not sell oil, no one will sell oil...”

(which is also a way to admit that the US oil blockade does hurt Iran more than what Tehran likes to admit)

Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours: Oman lane is dead.

Visible traffic continues to be nonexistent in the Oman lane, but the Iranians reported that 3 vessels attempted to transit with US escorts only to have one get attacked. The other 2 turned around. 

With the Bab al-Mandeb closure now in force by the Houthis, Fujairah enforcement is the most likely next escalation path. 

Despite tankers being attacked during their transits through the Oman lane, some brave shipowners and captains are still trying, which means the enforcement has to go to the root cause: Fujairah.

I guess we will see where this is all going, but from an oil math standpoint, this is the endgame now.

Video is from @MarineTraffic

Escalation ladder on the oil math:

- Oman lane: 6 million b/d, 2 million b/d UAE, 4 million b/d Saudi/Kuwait/Iraq

- Bab al-Mandeb: 5 million b/d, 4 in crude, 1 in product. Bypass through Suez, loading speed drops (1.5 million b/d)

- Fujairah: 2 million b/d, zero ship to ship

Oman lane and BAM already in play, 7.5 million b/d of crude offline. 

Fujairah is the last card. If that’s played, it’s game over. 

No idea why tanker owners still risk going there. It’s like shooting fish in a barrel. Terrible risk to reward ratio.

The operator of Vietnam's largest oil refinery, Nghi Son Refinery and Petrochemical LLC, has secured sufficient crude oil for its operations through to the end of September, state energy firm Petrovietnam said on Thursday.

The refinery can now process 10 types of crude oil, Petrovietnam said in a statement. The facility was originally designed to process only Kuwait Export Crude.

US oil refineries run at breakneck speeds as wars choke fuel supplies
High utilisation rates create risks of outages at a time when fuel prices are already up sharply
#oott
https://t.co/9RFfaZ0QlU

American fuelmakers are gorging on the most Venezuelan oil in almost a decade as the threat of wildfires in Canada risks disrupting supplies at a time when refineries from Texas to Louisiana are running at full tilt.

Imports from Venezuela are on pace to hit 804,000 barrels a day for cargoes arriving in July, the highest level since 2017, data compiled by Bloomberg show. US sanctions effectively shut off imports in 2019 before ramping up this year following the US ouster of the country’s former president.

Malaysian energy firm Petroleum Sarawak Berhad, or Petros, has achieved first oil at a field near Miri, marking the return of onshore production in Sarawak state after more than 50 years, national news agency Bernama reported, quoting state premier Abang Johari Openg.

My base case for Chinese crude import demand next month is more or less Chinese crude import demand over the past month—down ~5 MMbpd vs Dec-Feb pre-war average.
